questions.question: Ann has demand q_A=20-2P for P≤10 and 0 otherwise. Ben has demand q_B=12-P for P≤12 and 0 otherwise. What is market demand at P=$6, and what does this quantity represent?

choices.A: 14 units; the average of Ann's and Ben's individual demands.
choices.B: 20 units; the horizontal sum of individual quantities demanded at $6.
choices.C: 18 units; the vertical sum of their willingness to pay.
choices.D: 26 units; total willingness to pay at $6.

questions.explanation: At P=6, Ann demands 20-12=8 and Ben demands 12-6=6, so market demand is 14, not 20. The correct answer should be '14 units; the horizontal sum.' A has the right number but wrong interpretation. C uses a wrong quantity and vertical aggregation. D confuses quantity with willingness to pay.

questions.question: Three students have demands for notebooks: q_1=10-P, q_2=8-P, and q_3=6-P, each truncated at zero. At P=$4, what is market demand, and how should it be interpreted?

choices.A: 12 notebooks; the sum of quantities each student wants at $4.
choices.B: 8 notebooks; the demand of the highest-WTP student.
choices.C: 18 notebooks; the sum of intercepts minus price once.

questions.explanation: At P=4, demands are 6, 4, and 2, so market demand is 12. B ignores two consumers. C subtracts price only once instead of for each demand. D averages or confuses price with quantity.

questions.question: At every price, 100 identical new consumers enter a market. Each new consumer has demand q=5-0.5P for P≤10. What happens to market demand at P=$4, and what is the mechanism?

choices.A: It increases by 300 units because market demand horizontally adds the new consumers' quantities at that price.
choices.B: It increases by 100 units because each new consumer counts as one unit.
choices.C: It decreases by 300 units because more consumers make goods scarce.
choices.D: It increases by 500 units because each consumer's intercept is 5.

questions.explanation: Each new consumer demands 5-0.5(4)=3 units, so 100 consumers add 300 units. The mechanism is horizontal aggregation across consumers. B counts people rather than quantities. C invents a supply effect. D uses zero-price demand rather than demand at P=4.

questions.question: Individual demand for an app is q=12-P. There are initially 50 identical users. After an update, each user's demand becomes q=16-P. At P=$8, how does market demand change, and why?

choices.A: It rises by 200 units because each user's marginal willingness to pay shifts outward, increasing quantity by 4 at the same price.
choices.B: It rises by 4 units because only individual demand changes.
choices.C: It stays the same because the number of users is unchanged.
choices.D: It rises by 400 units because price is subtracted from the market intercept once.

questions.explanation: Old individual demand is 4, new is 8, so each user demands 4 more. Across 50 users, market demand rises by 200. B forgets aggregation. C ignores the individual demand shift. D uses an incorrect aggregation formula.

questions.question: Two consumers have downward-sloping individual demand curves. At a price of $10, consumer A demands 3 units and consumer B demands 5 units. What point must lie on the market demand curve?

choices.A: P=$20, Q=8 because prices are added vertically.
choices.B: P=$10, Q=8 because quantities are added horizontally at the same price.
choices.C: P=$10, Q=2 because the difference in quantities measures market demand.
choices.D: P=$15, Q=4 because market demand averages individual demand curves.

questions.explanation: Market demand horizontally sums quantities at each price, so at P=$10 total Q=3+5=8. A vertically adds prices. C subtracts quantities. D averages instead of summing.

questions.question: Market demand has a kink at P=$12 because one group of consumers stops buying above $12 while another continues up to $20. What does the kink represent?

choices.A: A change in the number of active buyers contributing to the horizontal sum.
choices.C: A point where market price must remain fixed.
choices.D: A vertical summation of consumer willingness to pay.

questions.explanation: Kinks arise when some individual demands hit zero and stop contributing to market quantity. B is false; kinked demand can still be downward sloping. C confuses a curve feature with price rigidity. D describes the wrong aggregation direction.

questions.question: Why is market demand found by adding quantities rather than adding prices?

choices.A: Because at a common market price, total quantity demanded is the number of units all buyers collectively want to purchase.
choices.B: Because all consumers must have identical willingness to pay.
choices.C: Because adding prices gives consumer surplus.
choices.D: Because firms choose quantities before consumers choose prices.

questions.explanation: A competitive market faces one price, and each consumer chooses a quantity at that price; market quantity is the sum. B is unnecessary and false. C misstates consumer surplus. D is not the reason for horizontal aggregation.

questions.question: A campus bookstore wants to forecast total demand for calculators at each possible price using survey responses from freshmen, sophomores, juniors, and seniors. Which model should it use?

choices.A: Horizontal aggregation of individual or group demand curves at each price.
choices.B: Vertical aggregation of prices at each quantity.
choices.C: Equivalent variation for each class year.
choices.D: Marginal revenue from the bookstore's supply curve.

questions.explanation: Forecasting total quantity demanded at each price requires market demand, obtained by horizontal aggregation. B is not market demand. C is a welfare measure. D concerns seller revenue, not buyer demand aggregation.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes individual demand from market demand?

choices.A: Individual demand gives one buyer's quantity at each price; market demand sums all buyers' quantities at each price.
choices.B: Individual demand is always linear; market demand is always nonlinear.
choices.C: Individual demand is measured in dollars; market demand is measured in utility.
choices.D: Individual demand applies to consumers; market demand applies only to firms.

questions.explanation: Market demand is the horizontal sum of individual demands. B is not generally true. C gives wrong units. D confuses demand with supply or firm behavior.

questions.question: Market demand for smoothies is Q=100-5P. The market price is $12. What is total consumer surplus in the market?
questions.explanation: Inverse demand is P=20-0.2Q. At P=12, Q=40. Consumer surplus is 0.5×40×(20-12)=$160. B doubles the triangle. C uses quantity 80 from a wrong substitution. D uses price times quantity minus an incorrect area.
